source.title: Demo feedback session - Sales Engineering (what broke + talk track tweaks)
source.summary: Internal SE/AE feedback on the new demo tenant + talk track. Main issues: (1) reset is still brittle/slow and sometimes leaves stale keys or missing seed objects, (2) dashboards are intermittently empty until synthetic traffic job is run, (3) 429 throttling shows up during live “economics” toggles / scripted runner bursts, (4) occasional RBAC mapping errors for SEs joining last-minute, (5) EU streaming disconnect workaround needs to be front-and-center. Messaging tweaks: don’t lead with “Optimize” as a product name; lead with “production economics controls” and show one concrete lever. Reliability/fallback story should be a shorter module unless prompted; keep it as an objection path but have a 60-second version ready. Team agreed on a tighter 30-min run-of-show and a ‘backup mode’ discipline (screenshots + canned outputs) when Console is flaky.
topics: Recent demo failures: 429s, empty dashboards, streaming disconnects (EU), RBAC access issues
Reset and provisioning pain points (time, idempotency, key rotation, seed data drift)
Console tour stability: which screens to avoid, default time range, project selection
Talk track edits: “Optimize” naming, ordering of economics vs reliability vs security modules
Backup plan: recordings, screenshots, canned outputs, and when to switch modes
Operational guardrails: healthcheck, demo SLOs, alerting/escalation for demo Sev2
questions: Can we make the healthcheck output more opinionated (PASS/FAIL) with exact remediation links?
Should the demo runner throttle itself to avoid 429s, or do we raise quotas in demo tenant defaults?
What is the official guidance on showing audit logs live vs screenshots only?
For 20-minute exec demos, which Console views are mandatory vs optional?
action_items: Devon Price / Eng Platform - Confirm one-command reset path is truly idempotent and returns timing breakdown (target < 5 minutes) - Due 2026-01-29
Allison Grant - Update synthetic traffic job preset to run automatically after reset and pin a default 24h time range in demo dashboard links - Due 2026-01-26
Sean Gallagher - Propose demo tenant quota defaults + burst limits to eliminate 429s for scripted runner; document ‘what to do if you hit 429’ - Due 2026-01-28
Alex Martinez - Pin SDK versions used in demo snippets and add a short note in the talk track for streaming retry behavior - Due 2026-01-27
Soojin Lee - Replace “Optimize” phrasing in deck/talk track with “cost & latency controls” and add a single sentence definition for batching/caching - Due 2026-01-30
Markus Klein - Publish a ‘Console screens to avoid’ list + backup screenshot pack link in the SE run-of-show doc - Due 2026-01-24
Talia Benson - Schedule 2 dry runs (NA + EMEA) using the tightened 30-min run-of-show and collect timing notes - Due 2026-01-31
next_steps: Markus to push an updated run-of-show with explicit branch points (20-min exec vs 30-min technical vs 45-min deep dive).
Eng/SRE to align on demo quota baseline and whether we fix via throttled runner vs higher capacity.
PMM/DevEx to finalize talk track wording changes and freeze v1.0 for certification.
Add a strict ‘backup mode trigger’: if dashboards are empty after 60 seconds + one refresh, switch to screenshots and continue.
source.transcript:
Meeting Header
Date: 2026-01-22
Time: 4:05 PM PT
Duration: 47 minutes
Title: Demo feedback session - Sales Engineering (what broke + talk track tweaks)
Attendees: Markus Klein (SE), Avery Johnson (AE), Hannah Schmitt (AE), Priyom Das (AE), Talia Benson (PM), Soojin Lee (PMM), Alex Martinez (DevEx), Allison Grant (Obs/Alerts), Sean Gallagher (SRE), Dylan Brooks (Console)

---

[00:00] Markus Klein: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone. This is internal, just quick gut-check on the demo tenant stuff. What broke this week, what made you sweat, and what we should change in the talk track so we’re not setting ourselves up to fail.

[00:18] Talia Benson: And I’ll be taking notes for the rollout checklist. Try to be specific, like “screen X did Y” not just “Console was weird.”

[00:27] Avery Johnson: I’ll start. Biggest one: we hit 429s in the middle of the “production economics” section. Like right when we flipped batching and re-ran the scenario runner.

[00:39] Markus Klein: Was that Northstar or another one?

[00:41] Avery Johnson: Internal dry run plus a live one earlier in the week. It’s basically the same pattern. The runner does a burst and then the streaming call stalls and we get throttled.

[00:55] Sean Gallagher: Yeah, I saw the alert. It wasn’t an outage, it was just the demo tenant quota doing its job, which is like, not what we want on a sales call.

[01:06] Priyom Das: In the moment it looks like Redwood is unstable, because the customer doesn’t care that it’s “policy.” They just see errors.

[01:13] Sean Gallagher: Totally. Two options: raise demo tenant quota ceilings, or make the runner self-throttle and be more “human pace.” Probably both.

[01:25] Markus Klein: Okay. 429s goes on the list. Next.

[01:29] Hannah Schmitt: Dashboards. Again. I know there’s work happening, but twice I clicked into the cost breakdown view and it’s just… empty charts. Then we do the awkward “maybe the time range” dance.

[01:44] Dylan Brooks: Was it empty-empty, like no data points, or did it show the empty state component?

[01:49] Hannah Schmitt: It showed graphs with no lines. Like it looks like you’re doing something wrong, not like “system is loading.”

[01:57] Allison Grant: That’s usually the synthetic traffic job not running recently enough. Or it ran but ingested into the wrong project. We have that “demo-seed” vs “demo-main” confusion.

[02:10] Markus Klein: Yeah, we need the dashboard links in the talk track to be pinned to the right project. Like literally a saved URL.

[02:17] Dylan Brooks: We can also default the time range to 24 hours in those saved links. A bunch of people end up in 15 minutes and there’s nothing.

[02:27] Talia Benson: Is that a product thing or a “demo link” thing?

[02:30] Dylan Brooks: Both. But for v1, demo links is fastest.

[02:36] Avery Johnson: Also, I shouldn’t have to remember to run a job before a demo. It needs to just be there.

[02:42] Allison Grant: Agree. If we hook it into reset, then at least after reset you’re guaranteed fresh traffic.

[02:50] Markus Klein: Great. Add: traffic generator runs after reset automatically, and we standardize the project.

[02:57] Soojin Lee: From a story standpoint, the empty dashboard moment is the worst possible break because it undermines the “observability is first class” claim.

[03:07] Markus Klein: Yep. Okay, other failures.

[03:10] Priyom Das: Access. I had an SE join last minute—like literally 3 minutes before the call—and they couldn’t see the org. RBAC mapping. We burned ten minutes.

[03:22] Markus Klein: Was that the new role mapping or old?

[03:25] Priyom Das: I don’t know. It was “you don’t have permission.” Then we tried a different key. Then it worked. So like… not confidence inspiring.

[03:35] Ethan Park: (crosstalk, faint) sorry I’m not on this one—

[03:37] Markus Klein: Ethan’s not here, but yeah. We need the least-privilege keys without making it impossible for SEs.

[03:45] Talia Benson: For the doc: what exactly would have saved you? A pre-flight check?

[03:50] Priyom Das: Honestly, a “healthcheck says: your user has X role, you need Y role; click this link” would have saved it.

[03:59] Allison Grant: The healthcheck endpoint exists, but the output is too like… engineering-y.

[04:05] Markus Klein: Yeah it’s like 80 lines of JSON, no one wants that.

[04:10] Dylan Brooks: Can we make it generate a short report? Like red/green plus one-liner fix.

[04:16] Sean Gallagher: That’s doable. Also we can treat RBAC misconfig as demo Sev2 because it blocks running the demo.

[04:26] Markus Klein: Okay, next. Streaming.

[04:29] Avery Johnson: EU region streaming disconnect. I’ve seen it twice now. It’s like the stream just stops mid-output.

[04:37] Alex Martinez: We have a retry pattern that helps, but the demo snippets don’t show it right now. The customer sees “broken stream” and it’s awkward.

[04:49] Markus Klein: Do we have a stable workaround?

[04:51] Alex Martinez: Yes: either run the live streaming in US region for the demo, or use the SDK helper that reconnects and continues. But we have to pin the SDK version, because there was a breaking change around streaming events last month.

[05:05] Hannah Schmitt: Also, if we’re doing EMEA demo variant, we can’t just switch to US and be like “trust us.”

[05:10] Soojin Lee: Exactly. For EMEA, we should have a backup screenshot for streaming output, and a line: “same interface, choose region; our demo tenant sometimes has a known issue, here’s what it should look like.”

[05:23] Markus Klein: I hate saying “known issue” on a call.

[05:26] Soojin Lee: Agree, but better than a failure with no explanation. Phrase it as “we’ll show the live call; if the stream is unstable today, we’ll switch to a recording.”

[05:36] Markus Klein: Cool. So backup mode discipline.

[05:41] Talia Benson: Let’s pivot to talk track. Soojin, you flagged some phrasing issues.

[05:46] Soojin Lee: Yeah. The word “Optimize” is causing confusion. People think it’s a separate product they have to buy, or they think it’s like a magical auto thing. When we say “Redwood Optimize,” customers go, “Wait, what is that?” and we lose two minutes.

[06:02] Avery Johnson: +1. I literally got stuck explaining the naming, not the value.

[06:07] Soojin Lee: I’d rather we say “cost & latency controls” or “production economics toolkit,” then show one lever: batching/caching toggle with token-level breakdown. That’s the aha.

[06:20] Markus Klein: Agree. We can keep “Optimize” for later, like pricing/packaging discussion.

[06:26] Priyom Das: Also we maybe shouldn’t show five knobs. Show one knob and the chart changes.

[06:33] Dylan Brooks: The Console view we’re using right now has a lot of knobs. We can hide some in the saved view, or at least scroll less.

[06:42] Markus Klein: Practical note: Less scrolling.

[06:45] Hannah Schmitt: For exec demos, we need a 20-minute version. The current deck/talk track assumes 30-35.

[06:54] Markus Klein: Yeah. We should define the “minimum lovable demo.”

[07:00] Talia Benson: Can we be explicit: what’s in the 20-minute?

[07:03] Avery Johnson: For exec: 2 minutes problem framing, 5 minutes live API (streaming + structured output), 5 minutes economics (one lever, one chart), 3 minutes reliability story (fallback as a slide), 5 minutes security narrative with screenshots.

[07:19] Soojin Lee: That ordering is good. Right now, we bury economics after too much platform talk.

[07:26] Markus Klein: Counterpoint: some technical buyers want routing/fallback early.

[07:30] Priyom Das: True, but it’s usually prompted by objections. Like “what happens when a model gets overloaded.”

[07:36] Sean Gallagher: Reliability should be an objection module. Don’t force it if they’re not asking.

[07:41] Markus Klein: Okay. We’ll keep a 60-second reliability module ready.

[07:46] Alex Martinez: Tool calling and structured outputs are the hero for app engineers. We should not rush that.

[07:53] Hannah Schmitt: Totally. The “compelling live example” needs to be better. Right now it’s kind of toy-ish.

[08:00] Markus Klein: What would be better but still safe?

[08:03] Alex Martinez: The support ticket triage example is good: model extracts fields into JSON schema, calls a “lookup_order_status” tool, then returns a structured summary. It’s realistic but synthetic.

[08:15] Soojin Lee: And it ties back to observability: you can show tool calls in traces.

[08:20] Dylan Brooks: Traces view is sometimes flaky though.

[08:22] Markus Klein: Exactly. Which screens are safe?

[08:25] Dylan Brooks: Safer: usage overview, token cost breakdown, error rate panel. Risky: traces if ingestion is behind, rollout view if the seeded rollout isn’t present.

[08:36] Allison Grant: Rollouts view is actually okay if the synthetic job is running. The issue is when it’s been idle.

[08:43] Markus Klein: So we gate those screens. “If data is fresh, show rollouts; otherwise show screenshot.”

[08:50] Talia Benson: We need a rule. Like a strict trigger so people don’t fumble.

[08:55] Markus Klein: Rule: if dashboards are empty after one refresh and 60 seconds, switch to backup pack. No more clicking around.

[09:04] Avery Johnson: Yes please.

[09:06] Priyom Das: Also reset. Reset is still too slow. It took me like 12 minutes last time.

[09:12] Markus Klein: What made it slow?

[09:14] Priyom Das: It’s like, reset command runs, then you wait, then you realize it didn’t rotate the key you’re using, then you’re in a weird half-state. And there’s no “done” signal.

[09:27] Sean Gallagher: That’s a design issue. Reset should return a clear success with what it did: seeded projects, rotated keys, kicked off traffic generator.

[09:37] Allison Grant: Plus verify ingestion. Like “last event timestamp” check.

[09:43] Dylan Brooks: Console also caches some selections per user, so people think they’re in the right project when they’re not.

[09:51] Markus Klein: Okay, so reset -> prints links, prints which project to open, and maybe opens it.

[10:00] Alex Martinez: And pins the code examples. Like the demo app should be pinned to specific SDK versions. We keep getting last-minute “oh the API changed.”

[10:10] Avery Johnson: Yeah, the night before demo you’re like “npm install” and now it breaks.

[10:14] Alex Martinez: Exactly.

[10:16] Soojin Lee: Messaging: we also need to tighten the “why Redwood” line. People latch onto “unified inference” or “production economics.” We should pick a headline and stick with it.

[10:28] Markus Klein: Field feedback is the economics angle resonates more.

[10:32] Hannah Schmitt: +1. Especially with enterprise teams asking “why not just run vLLM ourselves.” Economics + reliability story is the answer.

[10:40] Priyom Das: And the “dedicated” talk—be careful. Customers interpret it as “single-tenant hardware.” We need clear language.

[10:48] Soojin Lee: Agreed. We should say “reserved capacity with predictable throughput and isolation controls,” and then stop.

[10:55] Markus Klein: Cool.

[11:00] (brief crosstalk)

[11:03] Talia Benson: Quick round: what are the top three fixes to unblock weekly demos?

[11:08] Avery Johnson: Quotas / 429s.

[11:10] Hannah Schmitt: Dashboard freshness + default time ranges.

[11:13] Priyom Das: Reset speed and “done” signal.

[11:16] Markus Klein: I’ll add RBAC preflight.

[11:19] Sean Gallagher: And streaming EU workaround.

[11:22] Markus Klein: Great.

[11:24] Dylan Brooks: If we’re doing the ‘economics chart’ live, we should also have a static chart in the deck as backup. That’s a single slide.

[11:33] Soojin Lee: Yes. One chart, one sentence about token-level breakdown.

[11:39] Markus Klein: Okay. Let’s talk about “backup mode” artifacts. Do we have a link pack?

[11:45] Talia Benson: There’s a Drive folder draft, but it’s not curated.

[11:49] Markus Klein: I’ll curate. Needs: console overview screenshot, cost breakdown, rollout view, audit logs screenshot, VPC architecture diagram. Plus a recording of the core API scenario.

[12:05] Alex Martinez: For the recording, keep it short. Like 2 minutes, not 12.

[12:09] Avery Johnson: Also, can we have canned outputs for the structured JSON response? Like literally copy/paste if live call fails.

[12:18] Markus Klein: Yes. But we must label it “sample output” so we’re not lying.

[12:24] Soojin Lee: Right, we can say “here’s the expected output format.”

[12:29] Sean Gallagher: On the ops side, we should define what qualifies as a demo incident. If demo tenant is down during a scheduled call, that’s a big deal.

[12:38] Markus Klein: Agree. But for now, we just need it not to flake.

[12:42] Allison Grant: Alerts currently go to an engineering channel. AEs/SEs don’t see them.

[12:48] Avery Johnson: Yeah, we find out when it breaks.

[12:51] Allison Grant: We can route demo alerts to a dedicated GTM-friendly channel or alias, not #sales general.

[12:58] Talia Benson: Put that in action items: alert routing.

[13:02] Markus Klein: Next: do we show audit logs live?

[13:06] Soojin Lee: We should have guidance. Security team prefers screenshots unless the prospect explicitly asks.

[13:13] Hannah Schmitt: Because it can show internal user emails or whatever?

[13:16] Soojin Lee: Exactly. Even if it’s synthetic, it’s easy to overshare. Screenshot is safer.

[13:22] Markus Klein: Okay: default to screenshot, live only with caution.

[13:27] Priyom Das: Also the “Private/VPC” section—customers want specifics: data plane vs control plane, where logs go, can we bring our own KMS.

[13:36] Markus Klein: That’s mostly narrative, plus architecture slide.

[13:41] Soojin Lee: We should avoid deep “on-prem package” claims unless we know it’s in scope. Keep it high-level: VPC/private control plane option.

[13:50] Markus Klein: Yep.

[13:53] (silence)

[13:55] Talia Benson: Okay, I think we got the big items. I want to spend the last ten minutes on the new 30-minute run-of-show. Markus, can you outline the beats?

[14:04] Markus Klein: Sure. Beat 1: Discovery question or two—what they care about, latency/cost vs compliance.

[14:10] Markus Klein: Beat 2: Core API live demo. Chat completion with streaming. Then structured output: JSON schema extraction. Then tool calling, like “lookup_order_status.”

[14:23] Markus Klein: Beat 3: Production economics. Show token-level cost breakdown and one lever—batching or caching—flip it, re-run, show deltas.

[14:34] Markus Klein: Beat 4: Observability quick tour. Usage, errors, rollouts only if data is there; otherwise screenshot.

[14:42] Markus Klein: Beat 5: Reliability module—60 seconds: policy-based routing + fallback to compatible variants/regions. Only go deeper if asked.

[14:52] Markus Klein: Beat 6: Enterprise/security narrative: SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logs, retention, private/VPC architecture. Mostly screenshots.

[15:03] Avery Johnson: That’s good. We should bake in a “what do you want to see next?” prompt around minute 18.

[15:09] Hannah Schmitt: Yes, otherwise it feels like a tour, not a conversation.

[15:13] Soojin Lee: And language: stop saying “our runtime compiles kernels,” etc. Too deep unless they ask.

[15:20] Markus Klein: Agreed.

[15:22] Sean Gallagher: One more thought: if we hit 429, we should have a line ready. Like “this demo tenant is intentionally rate limited; in production we size quotas to your throughput.” But that’s still not ideal.

[15:35] Avery Johnson: Yeah, but it’s better than awkward silence.

[15:38] Priyom Das: And it can pivot into Dedicated/reserved capacity story.

[15:42] Markus Klein: Okay. But still, fix the quotas.

[15:46] Allison Grant: On dashboards: can we show a “last 24 hours” view always? It makes it look alive.

[15:52] Dylan Brooks: Yes. And we can add a banner that says which project you’re in, more prominent.

[15:58] Markus Klein: That would be huge.

[16:00] Talia Benson: Time check. We’re at like 16 minutes in transcript, but meeting-wise we have ~10 minutes left. Any other spicy feedback?

[16:08] Hannah Schmitt: Small thing: The demo tenant naming. “demo-main” and “demo-seed” are confusing. Can we just have “Demo - Sales” and then “Demo - Parallel 1/2/3”?

[16:18] Markus Klein: That’s part of parallel demos support. But yes, naming matters.

[16:22] Priyom Das: And keys. Please stop sharing keys in Slack. People do it.

[16:27] Markus Klein: Yeah. We need an access policy.

[16:30] Talia Benson: Noted.

[16:32] Alex Martinez: Last thing: the OpenAI-compatible endpoint vs native SDK. Some prospects ask. We should have a one-liner: “We support both; native gives you best access to Redwood features.”

[16:44] Soojin Lee: And don’t get dragged into a religious debate.

[16:48] Markus Klein: Exactly.

[16:50] Sean Gallagher: I’ll take the quota and reset reliability stuff back to SRE/Platform.

[16:55] Allison Grant: I’ll own the traffic generator + links.

[16:58] Dylan Brooks: I’ll send the list of stable Console screens.

[17:02] Talia Benson: Great. I’ll schedule two dry runs: one NA, one EMEA.

[17:07] Markus Klein: Perfect. Recap: fix 429, fix dashboard emptiness by auto-running synthetic traffic + correct default links, improve healthcheck to human-readable, update talk track to remove “Optimize” naming and shorten reliability module, and create a curated backup pack.

[17:23] Avery Johnson: And pin SDK versions.

[17:25] Markus Klein: And pin SDK versions. Cool.

[17:28] Talia Benson: Thanks all. I’ll send notes and action items.

---
End of transcript (auto-generated by Fireflies)
Notes: Minor transcription errors likely (product names, acronyms, and region references).

source.title: Controlled Failover Game Day Briefing (prod) - us-east to eu-west
source.summary: Pre-brief for the production controlled regional failover (us-east -> eu-west). Covered run-of-show, go/no-go gates, signal dashboards to watch, comms plan (internal + support + status page), and explicit rollback triggers. Confirmed limited blast radius (selected routes/tenants), dry-run output expectations, and who has authority to approve execution/rollback.
topics: Run-of-show and roles during the game day
Signals and dashboards (reachability, 5xx burn, latency burn, capacity/headroom, policy flips)
Automation controller modes (dry-run vs execute) + approval hooks
Circuit breakers, retry-after behavior, and streaming reconnect expectations
Dedicated pool drain sequencing + safety checks
Communications plan: Slack channels, incident-bot posts, Support guidance, status page template
next_steps: Allison/Sergio: final dashboard annotations wiring (automation-driven markers) before tomorrow 15:00 UTC
Victor: confirm cross-region probe coverage for us-east<->eu-west and baseline reachability in last 24h
Vanessa: re-validate eu-west headroom numbers against current baseline (last 7 days) and confirm safe-to-failover criteria
Devon: verify incident-bot summary formatting and links (dashboards + policy decision audit event) in staging
Rafael/Miles: publish final run-of-show + go/no-go checklist in #eng-sre and pin it
Dev/Support: send internal support note with canned responses + escalation path
action_items: Allison Grant: Add annotation stream for failover_controller events to 'Failover Signals' dashboard (due 2026-01-15)
Sergio Costa: Validate alert routing for burn-rate rules is paging correctly only for production tiers in scope (due 2026-01-15)
Victor Alvarez: Confirm any DNS TTL change rollout status + current TTLs and note known provider behavior risks (due 2026-01-15)
Vanessa Ortiz: Confirm eu-west capacity headroom threshold and share 'stop if headroom < X%' number in briefing doc (due 2026-01-15)
Peter Holtz: Reconfirm gateway retry budget / retry-after semantics are enabled for scoped routes (due 2026-01-15)
Devon Price: Dry-run output screenshot/template for incident timeline entry (due 2026-01-15)
source.transcript:
Meeting: Controlled Failover Game Day Briefing (prod) - us-east to eu-west
Recorded: 2026-01-14 17:05 UTC
Duration: ~47 minutes
Attendees (Redwood): Rafael Mendes, Miles Thornton, Mina Haddad, Ravi Menon, Ethan Park, Allison Grant, Sergio Costa, Victor Alvarez, Vanessa Ortiz, Peter Holtz, Devon Price, Tomáš Novák, Talia Benson, Dev Patel, Owen Phillips
Also present (leadership/product): Neha Kapoor, Sean Gallagher, Lauren Bishop, Nikhil Sharma, Marissa Cole, Paula Mendes, Chloe Martin, Nora Ahmed


[Auto Summary]
- Confirmed tomorrow’s production controlled failover will start in dry-run mode, then execute after explicit go/no-go.
- Blast radius is limited to selected Hosted API routes/models and a small set of Dedicated pools with pre-notified accounts.
- Agreed on “stop / rollback” triggers (reachability, 5xx burn, p99 latency burn, capacity/headroom, control plane errors).
- Reviewed comms plan: internal Slack, incident-bot summaries, Support macros, optional status page note if any customer-visible impact occurs.


[Action Items]
- See action_items metadata.


[Questions]
- What’s the exact threshold for “capacity exhaustion” gating failover vs just shedding load?
- If reachability probes are failing but in-region error rate is fine, do we hold or still fail over?
- Who is the single rollback authority if there’s a disagreement in the room?


Transcript

00:00 Rafael Mendes: Alright, I think Fireflies is on. Um, this is the briefing for tomorrow’s controlled failover in production, us-east to eu-west. Goal today is run-of-show, safety checks, comms, and very explicit rollback triggers. If we’re not aligned on those, we should not do the test.

00:18 Talia Benson: Quick note, I’ll capture timestamps and owners as we go, but please say your name when you jump in. The transcript gets… creative.

00:26 Miles Thornton: Yeah. And we’ll keep it crisp. The point is no surprises tomorrow.

00:31 Sean Gallagher: Also, leadership wise, Neha’s here for go/no-go criteria, but we want the operators comfortable and in control.

00:40 Neha Kapoor: Yep. I’m mostly listening. I want the rollback conditions to be unambiguous and I want to know who can call it.

00:49 Rafael Mendes: Perfect. Agenda: one, scope and blast radius. Two, run-of-show. Three, signals/dashboards. Four, comms plan. Five, rollback and stop conditions. Then quick Q&A.

01:04 Rafael Mendes: Scope: Hosted API multi-region routing and a limited Dedicated pool drain workflow. Not touching Private deployments. For Hosted, we’re only enabling the controller for the “chat-completions” route family on the models in the game day allowlist—Mina, can you confirm?

01:20 Mina Haddad: Yeah. Allowlist is: rw-llama-70b-prod-03, rw-mixtral-8x7b-prod-07, and embeddings aren’t included. Only the traffic policy objects for those routes. And only for tiers: dev, pro, and two enterprise tenants who explicitly opted in.

01:37 Dev Patel: Support side, those two enterprise accounts are the ones we flagged. We have internal notes ready. We’re not sending broad email blasts.

01:44 Lauren Bishop: Dedicated: we have three Dedicated pools included. Two are in staging-like low load, one is a real customer pool but they signed off on a short drain event window.

01:55 Vanessa Ortiz: For the Dedicated pool in scope, we validated headroom in eu-west to absorb the projected load even if drain is slower than expected. I’ll share the latest numbers after this.

02:09 Rafael Mendes: Great. Run-of-show. Tomorrow we start in dry-run mode. We will simulate the impairment signals first, confirm the controller produces the expected recommended action, then we flip to execute with approvals.

02:23 Ravi Menon: Just to be very explicit: dry-run will emit audit events and the “would-have-flipped” policy decisions, but it will not change routing weights. The policy engine evaluation is deterministic now, so the same signals should map to the same decision.

02:38 Jordan Lee: (audio) Sorry, Jordan here. Yup. Deterministic evaluation means order of signals is fixed and we also require corroboration signals for certain classes.

02:47 Rafael Mendes: Thanks, Jordan. On approvals: production execute is gated behind the approval hook. That means the controller posts an approval request in Slack and we ack it. Who’s the approver?

02:59 Tomáš Novák: On-call primary SRE for the window plus backup. That’s you, Rafael, as primary and Miles as backup, per the rota.

03:07 Miles Thornton: I’m comfortable being rollback authority if Rafael is heads-down. But I’d prefer we designate a single “stop” caller. Neha asked.

03:15 Neha Kapoor: Yes. One person can delegate, but in the moment I don’t want committee. Who is it.

03:21 Rafael Mendes: Ok. For tomorrow: Miles is the single rollback authority. If Miles says rollback, we rollback. If he’s unreachable, it falls to Sean. Everyone okay?

03:31 Sean Gallagher: Fine by me.

03:33 Mina Haddad: Works.

03:34 Allison Grant: Yep.

03:36 Rafael Mendes: Next: timing. We’re targeting 10:00 Pacific start. 10 minutes pre-flight, then dry-run. If dry-run outputs are sane, execute at ~10:20. Total execution window 30 minutes, then 30 minutes post-validate.

03:54 Talia Benson: I’ll put that into the pinned run-of-show doc.

04:00 Rafael Mendes: Pre-flight checks. Allison, can you call the “must be green” list?

04:05 Allison Grant: Sure. Must be green means: (1) region health service is ingesting reachability probes and service metrics for both regions, no missing streams. (2) Failover Signals dashboard loads; panels for reachability, 5xx burn, tail latency burn, capacity/headroom, and policy flips are updating within 60 seconds. (3) Burn-rate alerts are in “notify-only” for the game day allowlist, not paging everyone.

04:32 Sergio Costa: And (4) annotations. We missed that in rehearsal. We need the controller to emit an annotation event when it recommends action and when it executes. Otherwise the timeline is a mess.

04:44 Devon Price: Incident-bot will post a summary in #incidents and #eng-sre when the controller recommends or executes. But yes, dashboard annotations are separate; we’re wiring those.

04:56 Rafael Mendes: Victor, networking side, pre-flight?

05:00 Victor Alvarez: Yep. Cross-region probes must show green from at least two vantage points. We’re using perf-canary probes from us-west and eu-central, plus internal. If probes are red but service metrics are fine, that’s a specific scenario—don’t automatically fail over, we need corroboration.

05:20 Ravi Menon: Right. ADR says reachability is highest priority, but only if it’s consistent across probes and client-level symptom metrics. One broken probe shouldn’t flip us.

05:30 Victor Alvarez: Exactly. Also DNS TTL: changes are already in place. Effective TTL is 60 seconds for the test endpoint. But be aware some resolvers cache longer; so we shouldn’t expect 100% of clients to move instantly.

05:45 Peter Holtz: Which is why gateway behavior matters. If some clients keep hitting us-east while we’re shedding, we need to fail “softly” with retry-after and not just timeouts.

05:56 Rafael Mendes: Let’s talk signals and triggers. Miles, can you restate the trigger hierarchy and dampening?

06:03 Miles Thornton: Sure. Hierarchy is: (1) reachability / blackhole indicators. (2) 5xx burn-rate. (3) tail latency burn-rate. (4) capacity exhaustion. Each has dampening/hold-down timers. And most require corroboration.

06:18 Miles Thornton: For the game day, we’re simulating a regional impairment signal by injecting reachability failures and elevated 5xx, but we’ll do it gradually. The controller should not flap. Rate limit is max two flips per hour per tenant policy, plus global hold-down of 10 minutes after a flip.

06:39 Benji Okafor: Just to add, per-tenant safety limits are turned on. If a tenant isn’t in allowlist, we should see an “enforced_noop” in audit logs rather than action.

06:49 Rafael Mendes: Good. Now, what do we watch during execution? Allison/Sergio, list the panels.

06:55 Allison Grant: Panels: “Region Health Summary” (overall status per region), “Reachability probes” (percentage successful), “HTTP 5xx rate” segmented by region and route, “Error budget burn” for multi-region availability, “p95/p99 latency” by region, “Headroom” (GPU and gateway), and “Policy flips over time” with reasons.

07:20 Sergio Costa: And “Shed load / retry-after responses” panel. That’s important. We don’t want to melt the target region. If shedding spikes, we may be over-driving the failover.

07:32 Peter Holtz: One nuance: retry-after will show as 429/503 depending on path. Some clients interpret that differently. But it’s still better than hard timeouts.

07:41 Nora Ahmed: Console banner and status widget—tomorrow we’re not enabling customer-facing banner automatically. But if we see actual customer-visible impact, we can toggle the “degraded region” message for affected tenants.

07:55 Nikhil Sharma: Product stance: planned test shouldn’t show “incident” messaging to general audience. Enterprise-only comms unless we see real symptoms.

08:07 Rafael Mendes: Ok comms plan. Devon, incident-bot + channels.

08:12 Devon Price: Primary coordination is #incidents and #eng-sre. We’ll also have a side channel #gameday-failover-2026-01-15 for chatter. Incident-bot posts: (a) when dry-run recommendation happens, (b) when execute approval requested, (c) when execute happens, (d) when rollback happens, and (e) a final summary with links.

08:37 Owen Phillips: Support escalation: #support-oncall gets a heads up at start and at execution. If a ticket comes in, we tag it with “planned-test” and route to Dev Patel. We have a macro that says “we ran a controlled routing test, should be transient, here’s mitigation.”

08:57 Dev Patel: And please do not promise “zero impact.” Our wording is “limited, monitored, and with rollback.”

09:05 Marissa Cole: Also, for auditability, we’re capturing the decision evidence. That’s important for enterprise follow-up.

09:13 Rafael Mendes: Great. Now the core: stop/rollback triggers. I want these read out and agreed.

09:20 Miles Thornton: I’ll propose. Rollback triggers are if any of these happen for the allowlist traffic:
- Sustained 5xx > 1% for 2 minutes in the target region, attributable to failover traffic.
- p99 latency > 2x baseline for 5 minutes in eu-west for the scoped routes.
- Headroom in eu-west drops below 15% GPU available for more than 3 minutes.
- Control plane errors: policy engine evaluation errors, missing signal stream causing controller instability, or any crash loop.
- Dedicated: drain fails to progress (less than 20% drained after 10 minutes) OR customer reports impact.

09:58 Miles Thornton: Additionally, if we see unexpected spillover: non-allowlist tenants impacted, or any sign of cross-region data residency violation (shouldn’t happen), we stop immediately.

10:12 Neha Kapoor: I want clarity on “attributable.” That’s usually debated in the moment.

10:18 Allison Grant: We can approximate with correlation: policy flip timestamp + traffic shift panel + error changes. But yeah, attribution is fuzzy.

10:27 Miles Thornton: Fair. For the test, we can simplify: if eu-west starts erroring at those thresholds after we shift traffic, we rollback. Even if we can’t prove causation in 2 minutes.

10:39 Sean Gallagher: That’s the right bias. It’s a test.

10:42 Victor Alvarez: What about reachability probes? If they go red during the test, do we rollback or proceed?

10:47 Ravi Menon: If probes go red but client symptoms are ok, we don’t rollback just for that. But if probes go red and we also see elevated retries/timeouts, that’s a rollback or at least stop escalating traffic.

11:03 Rafael Mendes: Let’s capture that. “Probe-only failure” is not rollback; “probe + client symptoms” is rollback. Talia, note.

11:10 Talia Benson: Noted.

11:12 Peter Holtz: Another rollback trigger: gateway queue depth or circuit breaker open rate. If circuit breakers open > 5% for 2 minutes, that’s basically overload.

11:25 Vanessa Ortiz: Capacity side, I want to avoid chasing noise. Headroom metrics can jitter. Can we say “below 15% for 3 consecutive minutes” rather than instantaneous?

11:35 Miles Thornton: Yes, consecutive minutes.

11:37 Sergio Costa: And error budget burn. We should not exceed, say, 5% of daily budget in the test. Do we have that threshold?

11:46 Aisha Bello: (audio) Aisha here. For multi-region availability SLO, daily budget is small. I’d rather tie it to burn-rate alert: if we trigger a high burn alert (like 14x) for more than 5 minutes, rollback.

12:03 Rafael Mendes: Works. “Any high burn alert sustained 5 minutes” triggers rollback.

12:09 Neha Kapoor: And who’s watching what? I don’t want everyone watching the same chart.

12:15 Rafael Mendes: Roles tomorrow:
- Miles: rollback authority + overall decision.
- Rafael: execution operator for controller approvals.
- Mina/Ravi: routing policy validation and sanity check of weights.
- Allison/Sergio: dashboards + alert triage.
- Vanessa: capacity headroom.
- Victor: reachability/network.
- Peter: gateway/circuit breaker.
- Devon: incident timeline + bot.
- Owen/Dev: support intake.

12:45 Mina Haddad: Also Chloe and Nora on standby for Console toggle if needed.

12:49 Chloe Martin: Yep, we’ll be available.

12:52 Rafael Mendes: Let’s quickly rehearse the sequence. Step 1: confirm pre-flight green. Step 2: enable dry-run flag for allowlist. Step 3: inject impairment signals in a controlled way. Step 4: confirm dry-run recommends us-east -> eu-west shift with reason codes matching ADR. Step 5: request approval to execute. Step 6: execute shift gradually: 10%, 30%, 60%, then 90% if stable.

13:23 Ravi Menon: One correction: the controller by default moves to 50% in the first action, then evaluates. We can override to “gradual mode” for the test.

13:31 Rafael Mendes: Are we in gradual mode?

13:33 Ethan Park: Yes, for the allowlist config pack, gradual mode is enabled. It will do 10/30/60/90 with 2-minute evaluation windows.

13:44 Miles Thornton: Important: if any rollback trigger hits, we do not attempt to “fine tune” and keep going. We revert the policy to pre-test and we hold-down for 10 minutes.

13:55 Mina Haddad: Plus, anti-flap means we can’t flip back and forth quickly even if someone tries.

14:00 Rafael Mendes: Dedicated drain. Lauren, can you describe the drain step and stop conditions?

14:06 Lauren Bishop: For Dedicated, we’re draining a us-east pool and moving traffic to eu-west Dedicated capacity. The drain endpoint will cordon new sessions, allow in-flight requests to complete, and then progressively evacuate. Stop conditions: any customer ticket, or drain progress stalls, or eu-west overload signals.

14:28 Vanessa Ortiz: We also require eu-west Dedicated pool to be at least 20% headroom before starting drain.

14:34 Rafael Mendes: Great. Status page. Paula, what’s the plan?

14:38 Paula Mendes: If there’s no customer-visible impact, no status page post. If we see sustained impact beyond the allowlist, we use the “Regional event / routing issues” template and we post an “Investigating” update. For planned test messaging, we have an internal-only note.

14:58 Neha Kapoor: I’m ok with no public post unless real impact. But if any enterprise account asks, we should have a consistent explanation.

15:06 Dev Patel: We do. It’s the support macro and a short FAQ.

15:10 Rafael Mendes: Good. Now, explicit go/no-go criteria. Sean, read the leadership criteria.

15:16 Sean Gallagher: Go if: pre-flight checks are green, the dry-run output matches expected reason and recommended action, and eu-west headroom is above threshold. No-go if: missing signal stream, controller crash loop, alerts paging uncontrollably, or any other incident ongoing in either region.

15:38 Sean Gallagher: And we will not run the test if there is a known provider incident in us-east or eu-west.

15:44 Victor Alvarez: I’ll check provider status 30 minutes before start.

15:48 Rafael Mendes: Great. Questions.

15:51 Nora Ahmed: Streaming behavior. In rehearsal, streaming clients disconnected and didn’t resume. Are we expecting some disconnects tomorrow?

15:59 Peter Holtz: There may be a brief reconnect on redirect. We have the streaming reconnect fix in place for allowlist SDK versions, but not everyone. For the test, we should measure disconnect rate. If disconnects cause customer-visible errors beyond threshold, rollback.

16:16 Mina Haddad: And on redirect, some clients may not follow. That’s why we’re keeping blast radius small.

16:23 Neha Kapoor: What’s the “blast radius” actually. Percent of global traffic?

16:27 Allison Grant: For Hosted, allowlist tenants represent about 3-5% of production request volume in that route. For Dedicated, it’s three pools, one is low usage.

16:40 Neha Kapoor: Ok.

16:42 Ravi Menon: One more thing: audit logs. We will produce an audit event with fields: decision_id, trigger signals, evidence snapshot, who approved, and the policy object changed. That’s needed for post-test write-up.

16:59 Devon Price: And incident timeline tooling will auto-ingest those, assuming the schema isn’t broken. We tested in staging.

17:07 Sergio Costa: Alert noise. If burn-rate alerts start paging the whole SRE rotation, that’s a stop condition, right? We don’t want to create a secondary incident.

17:17 Miles Thornton: Agreed. If paging noise exceeds, say, more than two pages outside the game day group, stop and rollback.

17:26 Tomáš Novák: We can route the alerts to a separate policy for the allowlist. But yes, if we see it leaking, we abort.

17:34 Rafael Mendes: Ok, let’s do recap and close.

17:38 Rafael Mendes: Recap:
- Start dry-run, validate recommendation and evidence.
- Execute under approval hook, gradual weights.
- Watch specific dashboards and signals with clear owners.
- Rollback triggers: 5xx > 1% for 2 minutes, p99 > 2x for 5 minutes, headroom < 15% for 3 consecutive minutes, high burn alert sustained 5 minutes, circuit breaker open rate > 5% for 2 minutes, control plane errors/crash, Dedicated drain stall, any customer report, or any non-allowlist impact.
- Miles is rollback authority.
- Comms via #incidents/#eng-sre + incident-bot; Support macro ready; status page only if real impact.

18:25 Miles Thornton: One more nuance: after rollback, we do a 10 minute observation window and we do not re-attempt in the same day unless Neha and Sean explicitly ok it.

18:36 Neha Kapoor: Correct.

18:38 Rafael Mendes: Any final objections?

18:41 (crosstalk)

18:42 Allison Grant: None.

18:43 Victor Alvarez: All good.

18:45 Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll send the updated headroom sheet.

18:47 Devon Price: I’ll verify incident-bot messages tonight.

18:50 Rafael Mendes: Great. Talia will send notes and pin the checklist. Thanks all.


[End of transcript]

Note: Transcript contains minor attribution errors and misheard terms (e.g., model names, acronyms). Refer to pinned run-of-show doc for authoritative steps and thresholds.

title: Controlled Failover Game Day Briefing (prod) - us-east to eu-west
preview: Pre-brief for the production controlled regional failover (us-east -> eu-west). Covered run-of-show, go/no-go gates, signal dashboards to watch, comms plan (internal + support + status page), and explicit rollback triggers. Confirmed limited blast radius (selected routes/tenants), dry-run output expectations, and who has authority to approve execution/rollback.
text: Pre-brief for the production controlled regional failover (us-east -> eu-west). Covered run-of-show, go/no-go gates, signal dashboards to watch, comms plan (internal + support + status page), and explicit rollback triggers. Confirmed limited blast radius (selected routes/tenants), dry-run output expectations, and who has authority to approve execution/rollback.
